summary: DHH argues that open source projects are not democracies and using open source software doesn't entitle users to influence project direction. He contends that the word 'community' misleadingly implies egalitarian participation, when in reality projects are run by small groups of elite contributors who do the actual work. He defends this hierarchy as morally correct — productive effort should be the basis of power. He suggests 'ecosystem' as a better metaphor than 'community,' and frames open source as a gift exchange rather than an entitlement program.
key_points: The term 'community' creates false expectations of democratic governance in open source projects
Open source elitism is good — projects should be driven by those who demonstrate dedication and competence through actual work
Open source burnout is often caused by user entitlement, not lack of compensation
The democratic ideal in open source is fulfilled through freedom to fork and choose alternatives, not through voting
Open source is better understood as a gift exchange or ecosystem than a community
money_quotes: Using open source software does not entitle you to a vote on the direction of the project.
Elitism is good, when it comes to open source. You absolutely want projects to be driven by the people who show up to do the work, demonstrate their superior dedication and competence.
Productive effort is the correct moral basis of power in these projects.
It is not, and never will be, one user, one vote. That is, it will never be democratic. And this is good!
Don't like how they're running a given project? Use one of the usual countless alternatives. Or start your own!
When people talk about open source burnout, it's often related to this entitlement syndrome. Although it's frequently misdiagnosed as a problem of compensation.
Ecosystems aren't egalitarian. There are big fish and little fish. Sometimes the relationships are symbiotic, but they're also potentially parasitic.
Open source is first and foremost a method of collaboration between programmers who show up to do the work. Not an entitlement program for petulant users.
key_insight: Open source governance is rightfully meritocratic, not democratic — the people who do the work earn the authority, and the gift of free software is the only entitlement users receive.
